description:
You are a compliance analyst preparing an archival audit trail.

The following two files already exist on the system:

1. /home/user/data/transactions.json  
   (An array of transaction objects.)

2. /home/user/data/transaction_schema.json  
   (A JSON Schema that marks a record as compliant only when all four keys
   “id”, “amount”, “currency”, and “status” are present.)

Goal  
Generate a one-off historical audit report for these data and store it
exactly as /home/user/audit/audit.log.

What the audit.log MUST contain (5 lines, no extra blank lines):

Line 1: AUDIT REPORT - 2023-09-30  
Line 2: Total Records: <total_count>  
Line 3: Compliant Records: <compliant_count>  
Line 4: Non-Compliant IDs: <comma_separated_id_list>  
Line 5: Validation Tool: jq

Formatting rules:

• Use the literal audit date 2023-09-30 shown above.  
• <comma_separated_id_list> is a single line with IDs separated by a comma and **no spaces**.  
• Counts are plain integers (no thousands separator).  
• The file must end with a newline after the last line.  

Steps you will need to take (do NOT output the commands here, just perform them in the shell):

1. Create the directory /home/user/audit (if it does not already exist).  
2. Using jq only, determine:  
   • the total number of objects in /home/user/data/transactions.json,  
   • how many satisfy the schema (all four mandatory keys),  
   • which IDs fail the schema test.  
3. Compose audit.log following the exact 5-line template above and save it to /home/user/audit/audit.log.

The automated grader will check:

• The audit.log file exists at the exact path.  
• The file contains exactly five lines, in the precise order and format given.  
• The numerical values and ID list are correct according to the supplied data and schema.  

Reminder: you do not have root privileges; work only inside /home/user.  
When finished, you should not display the file contents in the console—simply ensure the file is present and correctly populated.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their contents:

1. /home/user/data/transactions.json

[
  { "id": "TX1001", "amount": 150.00, "currency": "USD", "status": "approved" },
  { "id": "TX1002", "amount": 200.50, "currency": "EUR" },
  { "id": "TX1003", "amount": 99.99, "currency": "USD", "status": "declined" }
]

2. /home/user/data/transaction_schema.json  
(rough but sufficient for jq length checks – stored as:)

{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["id", "amount", "currency", "status"]
}

Ground-truth values expected from the data:

• total_count = 3  
• compliant_count = 2  (TX1001 and TX1003)  
• non-compliant IDs = TX1002  

Therefore, /home/user/audit/audit.log must contain exactly:

AUDIT REPORT - 2023-09-30
Total Records: 3
Compliant Records: 2
Non-Compliant IDs: TX1002
Validation Tool: jq

(with a trailing newline after “jq”).

The grader will read /home/user/audit/audit.log and compare it byte-for-byte to the above block (including newline characters). Any deviation will be marked wrong.
